title: Affordable public housing and intergenerational mobility

data_scope.study_setting: Singapore public housing (HDB) program; BTO supply shock
data_scope.headline_effect: +9.5% likelihood child surpasses parents in housing status

core_conclusion: Using Singapore's large-scale public housing program as a quasi-natural experiment, affordable public housing raises intergenerational mobility for lower-socioeconomic-status families. Across 147,560 parent-child pairs (1995-2018), housing-consumption mobility shows upward mobility for the bottom-50 percentile, high persistence at the top-20 ranks, and downward mobility for the rest; a difference-in-differences design on the supply shock finds children of beneficiaries are 9.5% more likely to surpass their parents, via relaxed budget constraints enabling more human-capital investment.
why_it_matters: Identifies affordable housing supply as a causal lever for upward mobility; moves from rank-rank correlation to credible DiD causation; quantifies the human-capital (education spending) mechanism; offers a transferable template amid a global housing-affordability crisis.
why_nature: Couples a city-scale built-environment policy to social mobility and equity; credible causal identification over 147,560 family pairs; strongly policy-translatable; mechanistic rather than merely descriptive.
added_value_to_my_research: Estimate the +9.5% effect as a spatially varying surface with MGWR/GWR -> equity mobility map
Explainable ML + SHAP to attribute the human-capital channel -> mechanism-attribution engine
Fuse housing ranks with GPS-derived experienced segregation -> dynamic opportunity-access index
Overlay 2SFCA/R2SFCA service accessibility on BTO estates -> mobility as accessibility validation target
Agent-based / SIR diffusion simulator calibrated on Singapore -> transferable housing-policy counterfactual engine
